coredata.dc:title: Preparation of highly crystalline nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their application in sequential fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Xiamin
Man, Huasheng
Dong, Lijuan
Huang, Jianying
Wang, Xiangyang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been a new class of fascinating carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials. In the present work, new N-doped CDs with highly crystalline graphite structures are prepared from renewable precursors, chitosan and tartaric acid, and are well characterized. The prepared CDs are applied as a biocompatible fluorescent sensor for the sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA. Among various transition metal ions, Fe3+ can selectively quench the fluorescence of CDs. Upon the further addition of AA, the quenched fluorescence of CDs is then restored as Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ by AA, which can be utilized for the fluorescent determination of AA. A good linear relationship in the range of 0–150 μM of AA concentration is established with a low detection limit of 0.02 µM. Moreover, the practical applications of this fluorescent sensing method in measurement of AA in food samples are successfully realized with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent detection
Ascorbic acid
Chitosan

Fluorescence analysis of AA in food samples
UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) properties of carbon dots
Analysis of TEM images and Particle size distribution
Fluorescent determination of Fe3+ and AA
Fluorescence analysis of AA in food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in aqueous solutions (0.1 mg/mL) (inset: photograph of the samples excited by daylight and a UV lamp at a wavelength of 365 nm). (B) Excitation-dependent PL spectra of CDs. (C) Optical images of CDs solutions (1.0 mg/mL) under the excitations with different wavelengths.
TEM images of CDs (A and B) and the size distribution of CDs measured by DLS (the inserted plot).
FT-IR spectra of chitosan and the N-doped CDs.
Effect of pH (A), NaCl (B) and time (C) on fluorescence intensity of the CDs aqueous solution.
Comparison of relative PL intensities of CDs after the addition of different metal ions (2000 µM).
Recovery of AA in Commercial Fruit Juices.

head.ce:title: Preparation of highly crystalline nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their application in sequential fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Biotechnology, Zhejiang Gongshang University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Biotechnology, Zhejiang Gongshang University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been a new class of fascinating carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials. In the present work, new N-doped CDs with highly crystalline graphite structures are prepared from renewable precursors, chitosan and tartaric acid, and are well characterized. The prepared CDs are applied as a biocompatible fluorescent sensor for the sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA. Among various transition metal ions, Fe3+ can selectively quench the fluorescence of CDs. Upon the further addition of AA, the quenched fluorescence of CDs is then restored as Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ by AA, which can be utilized for the fluorescent determination of AA. A good linear relationship in the range of 0–150 μM of AA concentration is established with a low detection limit of 0.02 µM. Moreover, the practical applications of this fluorescent sensing method in measurement of AA in food samples are successfully realized with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their first discovery in 2004 during the synthesis of single-walled carbon nanotubes, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a new class of fascinating carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials owing to their unique features, including broadband optical absorption, bright emission, good chemical and photo stability, high water-solubility, facile preparation, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility (Nekoueian et al., 2019). These features enable their extensive applications in sensing (Li, Chen, Gooding, & Liu, 2019), bioimaging (Ding, Zhu, & Tian, 2014), photocatalysis (Fernando et al., 2015), optoelectronics (Li, Rui, Song, Shen, & Zeng, 2015), biomedicine (Anwar et al., 2019) and nanotheranostics (Du, Xu, Fan, Sun, & Peng, 2019). Especially, the excellent biocompatibility and non-toxicity (Anand et al., 2019) distinguish them from traditional transition metal-based quantum dots and organic dyes (Lin et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2013), and make them particularly suitable for the applications in biological and food systems. In the past few years, a variety of organic compounds and natural products have been utilized as the carbon sources to prepare CDs with different photophysical properties (Anwar et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2014; Du et al., 2019; Fernando et al., 2015; Li et al., 2015, 2019; Nekoueian et al., 2019). As a natural abundant glucosamine polysaccharide, chitosan has also been used as precursors for the preparation of CDs (Xiao, Yuan, He, & Lu, 2013). The rich nitrogen content in chitosan makes it a promising candidate for the preparation of N-doped CDs that usually have higher fluorescence efficiency than the undoped CDs (Li et al., 2019). Doping with nitrogen or other heteroatoms is conducive to increasing the affinity of CDs towards analytes attributing to the enriched binding sites for the analytes (Vaz et al., 2017). Moreover, by comparison with the small molecule precursors, the usage of polymer-based precursors are beneficial to construction of continuous graphite structures in the CDs. The N-doped CDs prepared from chitosan have been employed for dye adsorption (Zhou et al., 2015), bioimaging (Moradi, Sadrjavadi, Farhadian, Hosseinzadeh, & Shahlaei, 2018), and high performance super-capacitor application (Tong et al., 2019). However, to the best of our knowledge, rare of the CDs reported in the literatures are high crystalline. The preparation of highly crystalline CDs remains to be a great challenge.

l-Ascorbic acid (AA), also named as vitamin C, widely exists in vegetables and fruits. As an essential micro-nutrient, it plays significant roles in many biological processes of human (Patil, Jayaprakasha, Chidambara Murthy, & Vikram, 2009). As it is well-known, AA cannot be produced in human bodies and can only be obtained from exogenous intake. In order to provide a necessary supplement and to serve as an excellent antioxidant, AA is frequently adopted as an additive in processed foods, drinks, animal feeds, medicines, and cosmetics (Du, Cullen, & Buettner, 2012). Lack of AA in the body will cause diseases, while excessive intake of AA may result in uncomfortable. Therefore, determination of AA is an important aspect to assess food quality and diet guidance. So far, some strategies have been developed to measure AA, including titrimetry (Apak, 2019), capillary electrophoresis (Peng, Zhang, & Ye, 2008), electrochemistry (Liu et al., 2013), chemiluminescence (Wang, Teng, & Lu, 2012), UV–vis absorption spectrometry and fluorometry (Tang, Wang, Du, Ge, & Chen, 2003). Among these methods, fluorometry has attracted increasing attention due to their high sensitivity, easy operation, and low cost. It has been reported to selectively detect AA by using fluorescent sensors, such as CdTe/CdSe quantum dots (QDs) (Yang et al., 2011), CdTe/CdS/ZnS QDs (Huang et al., 2014), carbon dots-Cr(VI) probe (Zheng et al., 2013), gold nanoclusters-PbS QDs (Zhao et al., 2015), MnO2 nanosheets/carbon QDs (Liu et al., 2016) and MnO2 nanosheets/7-hydroxycoumarin hybrids (Zhai, Wang, Yu, Wang, & Mao, 2014). Nonetheless, most of these fluorescent sensors contain toxic metal ions or organic agents, which are hazardous to the environment and food safety. Hence, the development of convenient, effective and environmental friendly fluorescent sensors for the determination of AA in foods and drinks is still in high demand (Apak, 2019).

Herein, we present a simple pyrolytic synthesis of new N-doped CDs with high crystalline by using chitosan and tartaric acid as the precursors and their applications in sequential detection of Fe3+ and AA. Fluorescence of the N-doped CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+ and could further be recovered by AA via reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+, which allows the CDs to be an effective, safe and environment-friendly sensor for the fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and AA.

Chitosan (CH) with the deacetylation of 91% and a centipoise viscosity of 60 mPa·s (1%, 20 °C) was purchased from Qingdao Honghai Biotechnology Company (Shandong, China). Racemic-tartaric acid was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). l-Ascorbic acid (AA) was acquired from Shanghai Bailingwei Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was produced through a Millipore water purification system (Merck Millipore, Germany). Other analytical grade reagents and solvents were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and were used directly without further purification.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded with a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were measured by using a RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan) in the range of 350–500 nm with a 10 mm optical pathway quartz cell, and the excitation wavelength increased by a 10 nm increment starting from 290 nm to 360 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were performed on a Nicolet 380 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Particle sizes were analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, UK). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was tested on an X-ray diffractometer (PANayltical Empyrean, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained with a Kratos AXIS SUPRA spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using a JEM-1010 (JEOL, Japan) at 100 kV at room temperature. NMR spectrum was acquired with a Bruker AVII-400 spectrometer (Bruker, Switzerland).

The CDs were prepared by a modified hydrothermal carbonization method with commercial chitosan and racemic-tartaric acid as the precursors. With stirring, 3.9 g of chitosan was completely dissolved in 50 mL of 2% racemic-tartaric acid aqueous solution. The mixture was put into a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave, and then followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 5 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the resulting mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was filtered through PES membrane with 0.22 μm pores (Micropore), and then purified by dialysis with a 100–500 Da molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) in 2 L ultrapure water for 48 h. The dialysate was finally freeze-dried to give light yellow CD powders.

Stability is an especially important feature of fluorescent sensors to ensure the accuracy and reliability during chemical detection or biological sensing. Therefore, the fluorescence stability of CDs under the effect of pH of the solution, concentration of NaCl and time was investigated. The detailed procedures are as following: The as-prepared CDs solutions (0.5 mg/mL) with different pH (2.0–12.0), different concentrations of NaCl (0–1.0 mol/L) and at different time were prepared and the fluorescence spectra were correspondingly collected at excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

In a typical detection experiment, the prepared CDs powder was dissolved in a phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL at pH = 7.4. Seventeen kinds of metal salts (K+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Cr3+) were dissolved, respectively, in distilled water at a concentration of 2000 µM. The fluorescence change was measured, respectively, at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm after adding 0.5 mL of each metal salt solution to 4.5 mL of the CDs solution to determine the selectivity of CDs towards the metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent determination of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative measurement of Fe3+ was performed at room temperature in a PBS solution at pH = 7.4. The Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations (0.5 mL for each one) were added, respectively, into 4.5 mL of a 0.5 mg/mL CDs aqueous solution. The fluorescence intensity was measured using the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. For the detection of AA, 1000 µM Fe3+ solution and 0.5 mg/mL of CDs solution were mixed in a PBS solution (pH 7.4). Subsequently, 0.5 mL of AA solution with different concentrations was added, respectively, to the above mixture until the final volume was 5.0 mL. After the solution was thoroughly mixed, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of AA in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of fluorescent sensing with the new N-doped CDs for real applications, the standard addition method was used for direct analysis of AA in the food samples. Typically, 0.5 mL of a sample solution was added into the probe solution containing CDs and Fe3+. The resulting solution was thoroughly mixed and maintained at room temperature. After 5 min, fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 320 nm. The content of AA in real samples was calculated according to a linear equation obtained from a standard solution of AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) properties of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent CDs were fabricated via hydrothermal carbonization by using a mixture of chitosan and racemic-tartaric acid as a carbon source. During the preparation of CDs, tartaric acid can react with the amino group on chitosan to form onium salts and the two carboxylate of tartaric acid link the sugar chain of chitosan together (Scheme 1S in the Supporting Information), which is beneficial to the formation of continuous graphite structures. The prepared CDs were firstly characterized by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The broad absorption peak in UV–vis spectrum of the CDs aqueous solution appeared at 285 nm, which is the characteristic absorption of N-doped CDs due to the existence of sp2 domains (π → π* and n → π* transitions) (Kailasa et al., 2019). When the CDs aqueous solution was irradiated with UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm, a strong blue fluorescence was observed (inset of Fig. 1 A). The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated to be 5.2% by using quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent; QY = 0.54) as standards. The obtained N-doped CDs possess better photo-luminescence characteristics than non-doped CDs (Vaz et al., 2017), which is owing to the nitrogen atoms with five valence electrons presented in the aromatic rings to bind with carbon atoms.

A wavelength-tunable emission was further observed (Fig. 1B). The emission wavelength changed slightly when the CDs dispersion was excited by the photo with the wavelength ranging from 290 to 320 nm. Meanwhile, obvious red-shifting of the emissions with decreased intensity was found when the excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 360 nm, which confirmed the considerable wavelength tunable ability of the CDs. The maximum emission intensity was furnished when the CDs were excited at 320 nm. As shown in Fig. 1C, the images at different excitations directly demonstrate the multicolor emission property of CDs. The λ ex-dependent PL behavior is probably because the functional groups, such as carboxyl acids and amines, act as the excitation energy traps and thus lead to the different PL properties by introducing different defects on the surface during the hydrothermal carbonization process (Hu et al., 2014). Notably, these CDs have a broader color range than the CDs in the previous reports (Anwar et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2014). The emission color ranges from blue and green to red, which is beneficial for multicolor imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TEM images and Particle size distribution

ce:section.ce:para: The XRD spectra of chitosan and CDs were carried out and compared to confirm the formation of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1S, chitosan has a main crystalline peak at 2θ = 20° and an amorphous hump at around 2θ = 11°. As different with that of chitosan, CDs have very sharp peaks located in a range of 10 ~ 55° of 2θ, indicating the formation of crystalline structures. The similar broad diffraction peak at around 19.2° and 23.9° respectively, corresponding to an interlayer spacing of 0.46 nm and 0.37 nm, which are larger than that of graphite (0.34 nm) possibly owing to the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups (Lu et al., 2015). The sharp peak at 26.2° is attributed to the graphitic structure with interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm, which also implies that the CDs possess high extent of graphitization (Miao et al., 2018).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy analysis was used to investigate the functional groups presented in the N-doped CDs (Fig. 3). After the formation of CDs, the characteristic absorption peak of chitosan at 3430 cm−1 attributed to the OH and NH stretching vibrations changed to two peaks at 3380 cm−1 and 3205 cm−1 respectively, and the absorption band at 2878 cm−1 belonging to CH almost vanished (Yang, Jiang, Xiu, & Huang, 2019). The characteristic CO stretching peak of acetylated amino groups of chitosan at 1654 cm−1 shifted to 1719 cm−1 which was attributed to the CO stretching peak of carboxylic acid groups in CDs (Chae et al., 2019). The absorption peaks of CDs at 1604 and 1401 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of CC, C-OH, and the bending vibrations of CN (Niu et al., 2016). The other sharp peaks from 1000 to 1200 cm−1 are attributed to the CO and CN stretching and –OH bending vibrations. The FTIR spectra of CDs showed vibrational peaks different from the corresponding chitosan, which provides further evidence for the formation of graphite species after hydrothermal carbonization.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further employed to investigate the composition and valance state of CDs. By using the XPS analysis, the atomic percentage of C, N, and O atom in the CDs was determined to be 61.60%, 6.25% and 32.15%, respectively. The CDs show totally different XPS spectra by comparison with those of chitosan (Fig. 2SA-C). As the XPS spectra of chitosan are presented in Fig. 2SA, the C1s core level spectrum can be decomposed into three contributions at 283.1 eV, 284.4 eV and 285.6 eV, which were assigned to carbon atoms bonded to the nitrogen of amino groups, one oxygen and two oxygen atoms, respectively (Nikolenko, Kuryavyi, Sheveleva, Zemskova, & Sergienko, 2010). Since chitosan has all of its carbon atoms bound to oxygen or nitrogen atoms, the C-(C, H) peak is not observed. The deconvolution of the N1s spectrum indicated that it was associated with aromatic N and nitrogen in an NH2 group, while the O 1 s spectrum exhibits the major peak at 532.3 eV. Nevertheless, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2SD) fits into four peaks at 283.7, 285.2, 286.7 and 287.5 eV, which are corresponding to CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively (Janciauskaite & Makuska, 2009). The graphitic core consisting of the sp2-hybridized carbons, along with the existence of carbonyl groups in high content could be responsible for the radiative transitions involved in the fluorescent emission of the CDs (Stan, Albu, Coroaba, Popa, & Sutiman, 2015). Moreover, obvious changes were observed in the N 1 s narrow scan (Fig. 2SE). A peak at 398.9 eV corresponding to CNC and a peak at 399.9 eV corresponding to OC-NH are presented, suggesting that the surface of the CDs is not only functionalized by multiple oxygen and nitrogen groups, but also can exist steadily in water owing to a large amount of hydrophilic groups on the surface (Pang, Zhang, Wu, & Feng, 2016). Meanwhile, two different peaks centered at 400.4 and 401.6 eV are corresponding to the pyrrolic-like N and graphitic-like amino N (Zheng et al., 2018). In general, amino-enriched molecules could improve the PL properties of the resulted CDs by acting as the N-doping agent and passivation agent. These studies showed that the formation of amide groups on the surface and the N-doping can co-exist during the preparation of CDs and both of them contribute to the improvement of PL properties (Hu et al., 2014). Furthermore, the O1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2SF) confirms these observations with two characteristic oxygen states of CO (530.6 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (531.7 eV), which are also observed in the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2SD). These results further demonstrated that the prepared CDs have a carbon core with graphite nature and plenty of functional groups such as –COOH, –OH and –NH2 containing groups on the surface, which lead to good water-solubility and might bring novel properties for sensing applications.

The formation of CDs is also evidenced by 1H NMR spectrometry in D2O (Fig. 3S). The peaks located from 7 ppm to 9 ppm are the typical characteristic of aromatic nucleus containing nitrogen atoms. The appearance of these signals due to aromatic or olefinic protons demonstrates the presence of sp2 carbons, which suggests the produce of CDs with graphite structures. It is further supposed that the CH2 protons in tartaric acid may be converted to the CH2 and CH protons, which is observed as the complicated signals at around 3 ppm. The peaks at δ 3.50–3.90 ppm can be assigned to the protons on the carbons that connected to carbonyl, amino and hydroxyl groups (Yan et al., 2017). Therefore, both 1H NMR and XPS spectra confirmed that the CDs contain hydrophilic nitrogen and oxygen-based functional groups.

Stability is an important aspect of fluorescent nanomaterials to ensure the chemical or biological sensing more accurate and reliable. As shown in Fig. 4 A, influence of the pH value ranging from 2 to 12 on the fluorescence of CDs was studied. The fluorescence intensity slightly enhances with the increase of pH in the range of pH 2–6, while the intensity of fluorescence gradually decreases with the increase of pH to more basic conditions, at pH 6–12. It can be proposed that the change of pH may cause electronic transition changes of p-p* and n-p* in graphite nanodomains by refilling or depleting their valence bands. Meanwhile, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity was investigated by changing concentration of NaCl (Fig. 4B). The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases slightly as the concentration of NaCl increases from 0 to 1.0 mol/L. The fluorescence keeps high even when the concentration of NaCl reaches to 1.0 mol/L, revealing the high fluorescence stability of CDs under high salinity conditions. As seen in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases only slightly even after 30 days. These results demonstrate that the prepared N-doped CDs have excellent stability, good tolerance at acidic conditions, and good photo-stability, which enable their applications in fluorescent sensing and imaging.

The coordination of metal ions with the fluorescence indicator can be used as the prototype of chemosensors. The potential application of the CDs in fluorescent sensing for metal ions was investigated by monitoring the changes in the fluorescence intensity with the addition of various metal ions (Fig. 5 ). Notably, Fe3+ (2000 µM) can apparently quench the fluorescence of CDs, while the addition of other metal ions (2000 µM) has no obvious quenching effect. The high selectivity of CDs towards Fe3+ could be attributed to the selective chelation of Fe3+ ions with the CDs through oxygen and/or nitrogen atoms in comparison with other metal ions. It has been demonstrated that the hydroxyl and amine groups on CDs could be easily coordinated with Fe3+, resulting in the selectivity for Fe3+ (Ahn et al., 2019). After coordination of CDs with Fe3+, the photo-induced excitation may release its energy in a non-radiative manner as it could transfer into the void d orbit of Fe3+ and finally showed as fluorescence quenching. The above results implied that the prepared CDs can be utilized as a fluorescent probe for the selective detection of Fe3+ ion without any modification and thus be a promising monitoring sensor for Fe3+ in food and environment science.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent determination of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The selective fluorescence quenching of CDs toward Fe3+ make it possible to quantitatively detect Fe3+. Fe3+ is an important metal ion in biological system because of its essential functions in oxygen transport, oxygen metabolism, electron transfer and many catalytic processes (Zhu et al., 2013). The quantitative relation between fluorescence of CDs and Fe3+ concentration was then investigated (Fig. 5SA and B). The fluorescence intensities of CDs at 320 nm gradually decrease when the concentration of Fe3+ increases from 0 to 2000 µM. Furthermore, the F/F0 displays a good linear relationship with the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0–145 µM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9964. The linear correlation equation is F/F0 = -0.002 [Fe3+] + 0.9918. Herein, F0 and F are the PL intensities of CDs at 320 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+. The detection limit for Fe3+ was calculated to be 1.15 µM based on the formula 3S/k, wherein S denotes the standard deviation and k is the slope of the linear regression equation. These results indicate that the CDs are an effective fluorescence quenching probe to quantitatively measure Fe3+ with a low detection limit.

It is well-known that AA plays important roles in life. As an excellent natural antioxidant, AA has good reductive activity and can easily reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+. The above studies have demonstrated that Fe3+ could quench the fluorescence of the N-doped CDs derived from chitosan and Fe2+ could not (Fig. 5), which enables the CDs-based fluorescence system to be a good candidate as “on–off-on” fluorescent switch sensor for the detection of AA. As shown in Fig. 5SC and D, the quenched fluorescence (F0) of the CDs by Fe3+ was gradually restored after the addition of AA. When 1000 µM of AA was added, the fluorescence almost recovered to the original value before the addition of Fe3+. It is reasonable to infer that Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+ by AA and the coordination between Fe3+ and CDs was interrupted, thus leading to the recovery of fluorescence of CDs. The quantitative investigation further revealed a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of AA in the range of 0–150 μM. The regression equation is F/F0 = 0.0059 [AA] + 1.0052 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9987, wherein F0 and F are the PL intensities of CDs at 320 nm in the absence and presence of AA, respectively. Based on the formula 3S/k, the limit of detection was estimated to be 0.02 µM, which is comparable to or even better than the other reported assays (Liu et al., 2016). In brief, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by Fe3+ and recovered by adding AA owing to the redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA, which make it capable of being an “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor for simultaneous analysis of Fe3+ and AA. Compared with the other methods that have been reported (Apak, 2019; Liu et al., 2013), the presented method does not need expensive equipment, complicated processes, and harsh conditions. In particular, this new fluorescence sensor based on CDs has a wide linear response range, low detection limit, high sensitivity and rapid response time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of AA in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility to apply the hybridized CDs-Fe3+ fluorescence sensor in the determination of AA in the practical samples was further tested. Four different commercial fruit juices and four different fresh fruits and vegetables were used to appraise the applicability and reliability of the developed methodology. With this fluorescence method, the amounts of AA in several commercial fruit juices (Table 1 ), including kiwi juice, mango juice, orange juice and peach juice, were estimated to be 11.65, 7.46, 11.76 and 3.54 mg/100 mL, respectively. Moreover, AA contents in several fresh fruits and vegetables (Table 1S), such as Longan, Australian orange, broccoli, and green pepper, were determined to be 41.26, 38.47, 52.85 and 39.35 mg/100 g, respectively. Recovery tests were performed to evaluate the accuracy of our method. Results for the contents and recoveries were summarized in Table 1 and Table 1S. The recoveries ranged from 86 to 104% with relative standard deviations of less than 3.5% (n = 3), which suggest this CDs/Fe3+-based fluorescence sensing system being a versatile, fast, precise, and reliable method for the analysis of AA in real food samples.

New N-doped fluorescent CDs were prepared from renewable chitosan and tartaric acid in mild conditions and were systematically characterized. As revealed by FT-IR, NMR, XRD, and XPS, the prepared CDs have good crystalline structure and rich surface functional groups, thus avoiding the tedious surface modifications for their applications. The CDs have been proven a promising biocompatible fluorescent probe for detection of Fe3+ and AA. Owing to the chelation between the surface functional groups of CDs and Fe3+, the fluorescence of CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+, but other metal ions almost have no effect on the fluorescence. A linear relationship between the fluorescence of CDs and concentration of Fe3+ was established in the range of 0–145 µM and the detection limit for Fe3+ was estimated to be 1.15 µM. The fluorescence of CDs quenched by Fe3+ could be remarkably restored upon the presence of AA, which was further utilized for the detection of AA. A good linear relationship in the range of 0–150 μM of AA concentration and the detection limit as low as 0.02 µM were found. Furthermore, this fluorescence method has been successfully applied in the practical determination of AA in several fruit juices and fresh fruits and vegetables. Satisfactory recoveries ranging from 86 to 104% with low relative standard deviation values of less than 3.5% (n = 3) were resulted. By comparison with previous methods, the presented CDs-based fluorescent sensing system possesses appealing advantages, including easy operation, high environmental safety, visual detection, wide linear range, low detection limit, good selectivity, excellent sensitivity and rapid responsiveness.
Xiamin Lv: Writing - original draft. Huasheng Man: Writing - original draft. Lijuan Dong: Software, Validation. Jianying Huang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xiangyang Wang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Research Project of Public Welfare Technology of Zhejiang Province of China (LGN19C200008) and the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LY20B040001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126935.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ahn et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Food waste-driven N-doped carbon dots: Applications for Fe3+ sensing and cell imaging
Materials Science and Engineering C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ahn, J., Song, Y., Kwon, J. E., Lee, S. H., Park, K. S., Kim, S.; Woo, J.; Kim, H. (2019). Food waste-driven N-doped carbon dots: Applications for Fe3+ sensing and cell imaging. Materials Science and Engineering C, 102, 106-112.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Anand et al., 2019
